QF.id: VB Concept and Role of Mutual Fund_17

question.text: What is a "unitholder" in a mutual fund?

answer.text: An investor who has purchased units of a mutual fund scheme
The fund manager who manages the scheme
The distributor who sells the mutual fund
The trustee who oversees the fund

explanation.text: **The Logic:** When you invest money in a mutual fund scheme, you receive "units" in proportion to your investment. The person who holds these units is called a "unitholder." Unitholders are the beneficial owners of the fund's assets and are entitled to their proportional share of the fund's income and gains. **The Trap:** Fund managers, distributors, and trustees are all different roles in the mutual fund ecosystem — they are NOT unitholders (unless they also invest in the fund personally). **Cross-Exam Context:** This is also tested in Series V-A (MF Distributors) under mutual fund terminology. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Your clients are the unitholders — they own the fund. You are the distributor — you help them buy and sell. Always remember this distinction and act in the unitholder's best interest.
